explanation.rule: The prefix under- is added to verbs and adjectives to mean 'too little', 'below a normal level', or 'insufficiently'. It contrasts with over- and often suggests inadequacy or insufficient effort.

groups.note: Means 'too little', 'below standard', or 'insufficiently'; often implies that something is not receiving enough attention, money, or effort

examples.definition: receiving less money than is fair or deserved
to think something is smaller, less important, or less difficult than it really is
to gradually weaken or damage something
lacking the advantages and opportunities that others in society have
to describe something as less important or serious than it really is

wordForms.definition: receiving less money than is fair for the work done
wordForms.example: Many workers in the care sector are significantly underpaid.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to give money in exchange for goods or work

wordForms.definition: not having prepared sufficiently for something
wordForms.example: He went into the interview feeling underprepared and nervous.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to make ready for a future event or task

wordForms.definition: to think something is smaller, less important, or less difficult than it really is
wordForms.example: Don't underestimate how much time this project will take.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to form an approximate judgement about size, value, or amount

wordForms.definition: to perform less well than expected or required
wordForms.example: The team underperformed in the second half and lost the match.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to carry out a task, duty, or action

wordForms.definition: to gradually weaken or damage something such as authority or confidence
wordForms.example: Constant criticism can undermine a person's self-confidence.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to dig beneath; to hollow out from below

wordForms.definition: not having the same advantages, opportunities, or standard of living as others in society
wordForms.example: The charity provides support for underprivileged children in rural areas.
wordForms.rootDefinition: having special advantages, rights, or benefits

wordForms.definition: to describe something as less important or serious than it really is
wordForms.example: The report tended to understate the scale of the environmental damage.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to express or describe in words

wordForms.definition: to fail to recognise or appreciate the true worth or importance of something
wordForms.example: Many employees feel that their contributions are undervalued by management.
wordForms.rootDefinition: the importance or worth assigned to something

wordForms.definition: not having enough qualifications or experience for a particular job or task
wordForms.example: She felt underqualified for the senior management position.
wordForms.rootDefinition: having the required training, skills, or experience for something

multipleChoiceExercises.sentence: Many workers in the sector are severely ___ for the hours they put in.
The research team ___ how long the project would take to complete.
She felt ___ for the senior management role and hesitated to apply.
Repeated delays are beginning to ___ confidence in the project team.
The official figures ___ the true number of people affected by the crisis.

transformExercises.sentence: Workers in that industry are routinely ___ despite long and difficult hours. (PAY)
He ___ the amount of revision needed and failed the final exam. (ESTIMATE)
The whole squad ___ in the first half and the coach was furious. (PERFORM)
The company's assets were significantly ___ in the annual report. (VALUE)
The programme was created to support ___ young people in the community. (PRIVILEGED)
